did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a deep residual network (ResNet), a deep learning model, to extract deep features for melanoma classification. It also utilized the Scale Invariant Feature Transform (SIFT) as a hand-crafted feature and combined these with classifiers such as SVM and RusBoost to obtain results in the context of melanoma detection using the ISIC 2017 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing an automatic melanoma detection system using deep and hand-crafted features. It details a specific methodology (ResNet, SIFT, SVM, RusBoost), describes experiments conducted on the ISIC 2017 dataset, and reports new quantitative results (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) comparing different feature combinations, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: The Impact of Replacing Complex Hand-Crafted Features with Standard Features for Melanoma Classification Using Both Hand-Crafted and Deep Features
brief_summary: This study investigates the impact of replacing complex hand-crafted features with standard ones for automatic melanoma classification. The authors utilized the ISIC 2017 dataset, which contained 2000 skin lesion images, to train and evaluate their system. They employed a deep residual network (ResNet) to extract deep features and the Scale Invariant Feature Transform (SIFT) as a standard hand-crafted feature. The extracted features were combined and classified using Support Vector Machines (SVM) and RusBoost algorithms. The results indicated that combining SIFT with deep features did not improve accuracy and even reduced it slightly for SVM. However, the deep-only solution achieved higher accuracy than state-of-the-art methods, demonstrating the effectiveness of simpler canonical approaches in melanoma detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for the binary classification task (melanoma vs. not melanoma). The dataset contained 374 melanoma images.

Used as the non-melanoma class in the binary classification task. The dataset contained 254 seborrheic keratosis images. The authors explicitly stated they were not interested in other classes of non-melanoma skin lesions.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into melanoma or non-melanoma (specifically seborrheic keratosis) classes.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is a 'binary classification, melanoma or not' using the ISIC 2017 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (melanoma vs. seborrheic keratosis) using classifiers (SVM and RusBoost) fed with deep features (ResNet) and hand-crafted features (SIFT). Performance is evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, recall, MCC, and F-score.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017 (International Skin Imaging Collaboration Challenge 2017)
datasets_used.description: A public dataset provided by the ISIC challenge containing skin lesion images used for melanoma detection. The study utilized a subset of this dataset for training and evaluation.

use.training: Used for training the deep residual network (ResNet) and classifiers (SVM, RusBoost) after data augmentation (totaling 16,000 images). The dataset was divided into ten groups for cross-validation, with nine groups used for training in each fold.
use.validation: Used for validation/evaluation via 10-fold cross-validation, where one group was held out for evaluation in each fold.
use.testing: Used for testing/evaluation via 10-fold cross-validation, where one group was held out for evaluation in each fold.
use.other: Used for comparison with state-of-the-art results from the ISIC 2017 Melanoma Detection challenge.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the performance of the classification models (SVM and RusBoost) by dividing the dataset into ten groups, using nine for training and one for evaluation in each fold.
To summarize the classification performance (Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, Precision, Recall, MCC, F-score) across the cross-validation folds.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using average accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and other metrics derived from 10-fold cross-validation without statistical significance testing.

ISIC 2017.notes: Results are averages after 10-fold cross-validation. The deep-only solution with SVM achieved higher accuracy (0.8324) than the state-of-the-art method (0.828). Combining SIFT with deep features decreased accuracy by approximately 2% for SVM and remained unchanged for RusBoost. Processing time measurements were taken on a system with a 12 core Intel Xeon processor with 64 GB RAM without GPU acceleration.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained on ImageNet ILSVRC challenge data

architecture_details.feature_extractor_output: 2048 features extracted from the last layer of the network
components: Residual blocks containing convolutional layers, batch normalization layers, and ReLU layers
Shortcut connections to bypass convolution layers

training_details.data_augmentation: Two crops and four rotations (45, 90, 135, 180 degrees) generating 8 augmented versions per image; scaling to 225x225

classification_or_output_head.type: External classifiers applied to extracted features
classification_or_output_head.feature_combination: Deep features (from ResNet) combined with hand-crafted SIFT features (Bag of Words with 20 bags) or used separately

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Deep Neural Network Ensemble (Matsunaga et al.)
preprocessing_techniques: Color constancy using the Gray world method to address varying illuminance
Data augmentation via cropping (two crops) and rotation (45, 90, 135, and 180 degrees) to generate eight augmented versions per image
Scaling images to a square size of 225 x 225 pixels

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied two crops and four rotations (45, 90, 135, and 180 degrees) to each input image, generating eight augmented versions per image. Cropping used the largest inner rectangles ensuring all pixels belonged to the original image.
Scaled the augmented images to a square size of 225 x 225 pixels to meet CNN input requirements.

conclusions: Combining standard hand-crafted SIFT features with deep features extracted from ResNet did not improve classification accuracy for melanoma detection.
For the SVM classifier, combining SIFT with deep features reduced accuracy by approximately 2% compared to using deep features alone.
For the RusBoost classifier, combining SIFT with deep features resulted in accuracy levels similar to using deep features alone.
The deep-only solution using ResNet features and an SVM classifier achieved an accuracy of 0.8324, which outperformed the state-of-the-art method (0.828) from the ISIC 2017 challenge.
The study demonstrates that a simpler, canonical approach using deep features alone can be more effective than complex hybrid approaches combining deep and hand-crafted features.
The deep-only approach had a significantly faster processing time (0.372 s) compared to the hybrid approach (0.7945 s).

The authors acknowledge limitations including the lack of skin lesion segmentation in pre-processing and the use of a limited dataset, suggesting that larger datasets and segmentation methods could improve future results.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a binary classification task (melanoma vs. seborrheic keratosis) and explicitly excludes other non-melanoma skin lesion classes, limiting generalizability to broader dermatological contexts.
The dataset is small (2000 images total, with only 374 melanoma and 254 seborrheic keratosis images), relying heavily on data augmentation (16,000 images) to train the model.
No skin lesion segmentation was performed during pre-processing; the model processes the entire image, which may introduce noise from surrounding skin.

The comparison with the state-of-the-art method (Matsunaga et al.) is not fully fair because the competitor used additional clinical metadata (age and sex) and more complex pre-processing, whereas this study used only image data.

The evaluation relies on 10-fold cross-validation on a single dataset (ISIC 2017 subset) without external validation on independent datasets, raising concerns about overfitting or dataset-specific bias.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) was reported for the performance differences between models.

Processing time measurements were conducted on a CPU-only system (12-core Intel Xeon, no GPU), which does not reflect typical deep learning deployment environments and limits the practical assessment of computational efficiency.
The deep features were extracted from a ResNet pre-trained on ImageNet (natural images), not on medical images, which may limit the domain adaptation capability of the features.
